source.submodule: Types of DOM Results & Other Selection Methods

topicTags: Types of DOM Results & Other Selection Methods

content.problemStatement:
This example shows how to select and update both single and multiple elements using JavaScript's classic DOM selection methods.

---
**Select the Header by ID**
```js
const headerDiv = document.getElementById('header');
headerDiv.querySelector('h1').textContent = "Selectors Learned!";
```
* `getElementById('header')` finds the element with the unique ID "header".
* Since IDs are unique, this always returns a single element.
* We then use `querySelector('h1')` inside it to select the heading and change its text.

**Select All Items by Class Name**
```js
const topics = document.getElementsByClassName('topic');
```
* `getElementsByClassName('topic')` finds all elements that share the class name `topic`.
* This returns an HTMLCollection — a live collection that automatically updates if new items with the same class are added later.

**Update Each Item in the Collection**
```js
for (let i = 0; i < topics.length; i++) {
  topics[i].textContent = topics[i].textContent + " (Updated)";
}
```
* Since `topics` contains multiple elements, we use a for loop to go through each one.
* We access each element using its index (`topics[i]`) and update its text.
---
**Your task**
- Task 1: Change Title Message: Update the text "Selectors Learned!" to "DOM Elements Updated".
- Task 2: Replace Topic Text: In the loop, change the line so that each topic's text becomes just "New Content".


Explore the code! Click **Run** to instantly see how it works, then click **Submit** to check if it's correct.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
